title: Ethylene Glycol & Methanol Poisoning
oneLiner: Toxic alcohol ingestion causing high anion gap metabolic acidosis + osmolar gap; rapid recognition prevents blindness (methanol) and renal failure (ethylene glycol).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.1):** leads to cardiovascular collapse, cardiac arrest. Finding: serum pH < 7.1 with high anion gap (>20), refractory to fluids.
- **Ethylene glycol:** acute renal cortical necrosis. Finding: anuric acute kidney injury (AKI) with calcium oxalate crystals in urine within 4–6 hours.
- **Methanol:** optic nerve oedema → irreversible blindness. Finding: sudden visual loss, “snowstorm” vision, or afferent pupillary defect (APD) on exam.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intoxication without ethanol odour (or double intoxication), slurred speech, ataxia
  - Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
  - Severe cases: seizures, coma, hypotension
  - Methanol: visual disturbances (blurring, photophobia, partial/complete blindness)
  - Ethylene glycol: flank pain, oliguria
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachypnoea (Kussmaul breathing)
  - Fundoscopy (methanol): hyperaemic optic disc, retinal oedema
  - Pupils: may be fixed/dilated in methanol toxicity
  - Hydration status: may be hypovolaemic
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  1. **Other toxic alcohol poisonings (isopropanol, propylene glycol)** – isopropanol gives ketosis without acidosis; propylene glycol (iatrogenic in ICU if propofol/lorazepam infusions)
  2. **Alcoholic ketoacidosis** – high beta-hydroxybutyrate, normal osmolar gap
  3. **Diabetic ketoacidosis** – hyperglycaemia, ketones, known diabetes
  4. **Metformin-associated lactic acidosis** – high lactate, no osmolar gap
  5. **Sepsis/lactic acidosis** – lactate elevated, no osmolar gap
  6. **Uraemic acidosis** – high creatinine, usually chronic
- **Splitter:** **Osmolar gap > 20 mOsm/kg** strongly points to toxic alcohol; if gap < 10, reconsider other high anion gap acidoses.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ABG/VBG – pH, bicarbonate, base deficit, lactate
  - Point-of-care glucose, ketones
  - Urine dipstick for oxalate crystals (ethylene glycol) – may be calcium oxalate monohydrate (“picket fence”)
  - ECG (rule out prolonged QRS/QTc from concomitant drugs)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, CRP
  - Serum electrolytes, lactate, ketones
  - Osmolality (measured) and calculated osmolality = 2xNa + glucose + BUN (if mmol/L units)
  - **Osmolar gap** = measured osmolality – calculated osmolality (normal < 10)
  - Methanol and ethylene glycol levels – send to specialist lab (results often delayed; treat empirically)
  - β-hydroxybutyrate (if available) to exclude alcoholic ketoacidosis
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head (if reduced GCS, seizures, or suspected head trauma)
  - Renal ultrasound (ethylene glycol – assess renal size and hydronephrosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine fluorescence – **NOT reliable** for ethylene glycol (false positives/negatives)
  - Serum formate levels (methanol) – rarely available
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **High anion gap metabolic acidosis** + osmolar gap > 20 mOsm/kg in appropriate clinical setting is diagnostic of toxic alcohol poisoning until proven otherwise.
- **Never-miss test:** **Paired ABG and serum osmolality** – if you don’t check the osmolar gap, you will miss the diagnosis in early stages when metabolic acidosis may be absent.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (ED):**
  - Secure airway if GCS < 8, airway protection
  - IV access x2 (wide bore), normal saline 500 mL – 1 L bolus (watch for fluid overload if anuric)
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (if present)
  - IV sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1 (1.26% or 8.4%, [check local policy] for dose and monitoring)
  - **Antidote ASAP:** Fomepizole (first-line) or ethanol (if fomepizole unavailable). Do not wait for lab levels.
  - **Activated charcoal:** only if ingestion < 1 hour and patient alert. Usually not indicated.
  - **Haemodialysis consultation urgently** – criteria: significant metabolic acidosis, renal impairment, visual symptoms, high serum levels (e.g. > 50 mg/dL methanol, > 20 mg/dL ethylene glycol), or osmolal gap > 20 despite antidote. Call renal team.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue antidote until methanol/ethylene glycol levels < 20 mg/dL and metabolic acidosis resolved
  - Monitor ABG/electrolytes every 2–4 hours initially, then as per response
  - Fluid balance chart, strict input/output
  - Dialysis if indicated – typically continuous venovenous haemodialysis (CVVHD) or intermittent HD
  - If dialysis not available, consider high-flow renal replacement therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If survived, renal function may recover fully or progress to chronic kidney disease (ethylene glycol)
  - Visual recovery (methanol) unpredictable – often partial/complete if treated early
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (adjust for AKI/hypovolaemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** dalteparin 2500–5000 units SC once daily (if no coagulopathy)
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until GCS > 13 and swallowing safe
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q15min initially, then hourly once stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole)**
  - Loading dose: 15 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes
  - Maintenance: 10 mg/kg IV q12h x 4 doses
  - After 48h (if ongoing): 15 mg/kg IV q12h (due to auto-induction)
  - Duration: until methanol/ethylene glycol levels < 20 mg/dL and acidosis resolved
- **Alternative: Ethanol (IV or oral) – use only if fomepizole unavailable**
  - Loading dose: [check local policy] (e.g., 7.5 mL/kg of 10% ethanol IV)
  - Maintenance: titrate to blood ethanol 100–150 mg/dL (22–33 mmol/L)
  - Dose adjustments: continuous infusion with hourly monitoring of ethanol levels
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Fomepizole: hypersensitivity, caution in liver impairment
  - Ethanol: significant hepatotoxicity, hypoglycaemia, interacts with metronidazole/disulfiram
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fomepizole: no dose adjustment in renal impairment (dialysis removes it)
  - Ethanol: no specific adjustment, but caution in liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose of fomepizole** while waiting for lab results – every hour delay increases risk of blindness (methanol) or renal failure (ethylene glycol). Also, **using the wrong ethanol concentration or rate** can cause hypoglycaemia or oversedation.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Methanol: optic nerve atrophy, blindness, cerebral oedema, coma, death
  - Ethylene glycol: acute tubular necrosis, cortical necrosis, anuria, AKI requiring dialysis
  - Both: severe metabolic acidosis, multiorgan failure, seizure, aspiration pneumonitis
- **Chronic:**
  - Visual impairment (methanol)
  - Chronic kidney disease (ethylene glycol)
- **Prognosis:** Mortality 5–20% depending on delay in treatment. Poor prognosis: pH < 7.0, coma, persistent anuria.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always calculate the osmolar gap in any unexplained high anion gap metabolic acidosis.**
- **Start fomepizole empirically** if there is any suspicion (history of ingestion, osmolar gap > 10, or high anion gap ± visual symptoms).
- **Call renal team early for dialysis** – do not wait for lab confirmation of levels.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Not checking an osmolar gap** – leads to missed diagnosis (patient may present early with no acidosis).
- **Using activated charcoal** – useless > 1 hour post-ingestion; may cause aspiration if GCS low.
- **Delaying antidote** while waiting for confirmatory levels – treat on suspicion.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening vision (floaters, field loss), new headache/confusion, decreased urine output, shortness of breath, or persistent vomiting.
- **Follow-up:** Nephrology clinic (for ethylene glycol) or ophthalmology clinic (for methanol) within 1 week; repeat renal function.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ITU / renal if:**
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - pH < 7.1 refractory to bicarbonate
  - GCS < 12 or seizures
  - Anuria > 6 hours
  - Any visual loss or APD
- **Immediate ICU referral:** ventilatory support needed, CVVHD requirement, persistent haemodynamic instability.

rawMarkdown:
## Ethylene Glycol & Methanol Poisoning
**One-liner:** Toxic alcohol ingestion causing high anion gap metabolic acidosis + osmolar gap; rapid recognition prevents blindness (methanol) and renal failure (ethylene glycol).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.1):** leads to cardiovascular collapse, cardiac arrest. Finding: serum pH < 7.1 with high anion gap (>20), refractory to fluids.
- **Ethylene glycol:** acute renal cortical necrosis. Finding: anuric acute kidney injury (AKI) with calcium oxalate crystals in urine within 4–6 hours.
- **Methanol:** optic nerve oedema → irreversible blindness. Finding: sudden visual loss, “snowstorm” vision, or afferent pupillary defect (APD) on exam.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intoxication without ethanol odour (or double intoxication), slurred speech, ataxia
  - Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
  - Severe cases: seizures, coma, hypotension
  - Methanol: visual disturbances (blurring, photophobia, partial/complete blindness)
  - Ethylene glycol: flank pain, oliguria
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachypnoea (Kussmaul breathing)
  - Fundoscopy (methanol): hyperaemic optic disc, retinal oedema
  - Pupils: may be fixed/dilated in methanol toxicity
  - Hydration status: may be hypovolaemic

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  1. **Other toxic alcohol poisonings (isopropanol, propylene glycol)** – isopropanol gives ketosis without acidosis; propylene glycol (iatrogenic in ICU if propofol/lorazepam infusions)
  2. **Alcoholic ketoacidosis** – high beta-hydroxybutyrate, normal osmolar gap
  3. **Diabetic ketoacidosis** – hyperglycaemia, ketones, known diabetes
  4. **Metformin-associated lactic acidosis** – high lactate, no osmolar gap
  5. **Sepsis/lactic acidosis** – lactate elevated, no osmolar gap
  6. **Uraemic acidosis** – high creatinine, usually chronic
- **Splitter:** **Osmolar gap > 20 mOsm/kg** strongly points to toxic alcohol; if gap < 10, reconsider other high anion gap acidoses.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ABG/VBG – pH, bicarbonate, base deficit, lactate
  - Point-of-care glucose, ketones
  - Urine dipstick for oxalate crystals (ethylene glycol) – may be calcium oxalate monohydrate (“picket fence”)
  - ECG (rule out prolonged QRS/QTc from concomitant drugs)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, CRP
  - Serum electrolytes, lactate, ketones
  - Osmolality (measured) and calculated osmolality = 2xNa + glucose + BUN (if mmol/L units)
  - **Osmolar gap** = measured osmolality – calculated osmolality (normal < 10)
  - Methanol and ethylene glycol levels – send to specialist lab (results often delayed; treat empirically)
  - β-hydroxybutyrate (if available) to exclude alcoholic ketoacidosis
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head (if reduced GCS, seizures, or suspected head trauma)
  - Renal ultrasound (ethylene glycol – assess renal size and hydronephrosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine fluorescence – **NOT reliable** for ethylene glycol (false positives/negatives)
  - Serum formate levels (methanol) – rarely available
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **High anion gap metabolic acidosis** + osmolar gap > 20 mOsm/kg in appropriate clinical setting is diagnostic of toxic alcohol poisoning until proven otherwise.
- **Never-miss test:** **Paired ABG and serum osmolality** – if you don’t check the osmolar gap, you will miss the diagnosis in early stages when metabolic acidosis may be absent.

### Management
- **Immediate (ED):**
  - Secure airway if GCS < 8, airway protection
  - IV access x2 (wide bore), normal saline 500 mL – 1 L bolus (watch for fluid overload if anuric)
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (if present)
  - IV sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1 (1.26% or 8.4%, [check local policy] for dose and monitoring)
  - **Antidote ASAP:** Fomepizole (first-line) or ethanol (if fomepizole unavailable). Do not wait for lab levels.
  - **Activated charcoal:** only if ingestion < 1 hour and patient alert. Usually not indicated.
  - **Haemodialysis consultation urgently** – criteria: significant metabolic acidosis, renal impairment, visual symptoms, high serum levels (e.g. > 50 mg/dL methanol, > 20 mg/dL ethylene glycol), or osmolal gap > 20 despite antidote. Call renal team.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue antidote until methanol/ethylene glycol levels < 20 mg/dL and metabolic acidosis resolved
  - Monitor ABG/electrolytes every 2–4 hours initially, then as per response
  - Fluid balance chart, strict input/output
  - Dialysis if indicated – typically continuous venovenous haemodialysis (CVVHD) or intermittent HD
  - If dialysis not available, consider high-flow renal replacement therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If survived, renal function may recover fully or progress to chronic kidney disease (ethylene glycol)
  - Visual recovery (methanol) unpredictable – often partial/complete if treated early
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (adjust for AKI/hypovolaemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** dalteparin 2500–5000 units SC once daily (if no coagulopathy)
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until GCS > 13 and swallowing safe
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q15min initially, then hourly once stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole)**
  - Loading dose: 15 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes
  - Maintenance: 10 mg/kg IV q12h x 4 doses
  - After 48h (if ongoing): 15 mg/kg IV q12h (due to auto-induction)
  - Duration: until methanol/ethylene glycol levels < 20 mg/dL and acidosis resolved
- **Alternative: Ethanol (IV or oral) – use only if fomepizole unavailable**
  - Loading dose: [check local policy] (e.g., 7.5 mL/kg of 10% ethanol IV)
  - Maintenance: titrate to blood ethanol 100–150 mg/dL (22–33 mmol/L)
  - Dose adjustments: continuous infusion with hourly monitoring of ethanol levels
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Fomepizole: hypersensitivity, caution in liver impairment
  - Ethanol: significant hepatotoxicity, hypoglycaemia, interacts with metronidazole/disulfiram
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fomepizole: no dose adjustment in renal impairment (dialysis removes it)
  - Ethanol: no specific adjustment, but caution in liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose of fomepizole** while waiting for lab results – every hour delay increases risk of blindness (methanol) or renal failure (ethylene glycol). Also, **using the wrong ethanol concentration or rate** can cause hypoglycaemia or oversedation.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Methanol: optic nerve atrophy, blindness, cerebral oedema, coma, death
  - Ethylene glycol: acute tubular necrosis, cortical necrosis, anuria, AKI requiring dialysis
  - Both: severe metabolic acidosis, multiorgan failure, seizure, aspiration pneumonitis
- **Chronic:**
  - Visual impairment (methanol)
  - Chronic kidney disease (ethylene glycol)
- **Prognosis:** Mortality 5–20% depending on delay in treatment. Poor prognosis: pH < 7.0, coma, persistent anuria.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always calculate the osmolar gap in any unexplained high anion gap metabolic acidosis.**
- **Start fomepizole empirically** if there is any suspicion (history of ingestion, osmolar gap > 10, or high anion gap ± visual symptoms).
- **Call renal team early for dialysis** – do not wait for lab confirmation of levels.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Not checking an osmolar gap** – leads to missed diagnosis (patient may present early with no acidosis).
- **Using activated charcoal** – useless > 1 hour post-ingestion; may cause aspiration if GCS low.
- **Delaying antidote** while waiting for confirmatory levels – treat on suspicion.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening vision (floaters, field loss), new headache/confusion, decreased urine output, shortness of breath, or persistent vomiting.
- **Follow-up:** Nephrology clinic (for ethylene glycol) or ophthalmology clinic (for methanol) within 1 week; repeat renal function.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ITU / renal if:**
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - pH < 7.1 refractory to bicarbonate
  - GCS < 12 or seizures
  - Anuria > 6 hours
  - Any visual loss or APD
- **Immediate ICU referral:** ventilatory support needed, CVVHD requirement, persistent haemodynamic instability.
